title: Life's Chemistry May Be Inherently Geological
summary: A Quanta Magazine article reports that what appear to be biochemical processes may actually be inherent geological features, challenging conventional assumptions about the origins of life. This paradigm-shifting hypothesis blurs the line between geology and biology, potentially redefining how we search for life beyond Earth and understand life's emergence on our planet. The article builds on decades of speculation that geochemistry can spawn biochemistry, citing examples like geothermal processes creating stable energy gradients that manufacture organic compounds.
context: Abiogenesis is the natural process by which life arises from non-living matter. Geochemical processes that mimic biochemistry, such as the formation of organic compounds at hydrothermal vents, have long been studied as potential precursors to life.
discussion: Commenters noted this idea has been speculated for at least a decade, with references to abiogenic petroleum and excitement for missions to Europa and Enceladus. One comment raised questions about protein mass spectrometry to detect residual enzymes.
